 {"id":"wa-1xk9j","title":"Zellij layout engine analysis — pane topology, floating panes, stacked panes","description":"# Zellij Layout Engine Analysis

## Why this matters
Zellij has a sophisticated layout system: tiled splits, floating panes, stacked panes, and KDL-based layout configuration. FrankenTerm inherits WezTerm's split model. Understanding Zellij's approach could improve our layout restoration (wa-e6pq) and pane management.

## What to analyze

### Layout model
- How does Zellij represent pane topology (tree? flat list? constraint-based?)
- How are splits calculated and resized?
- How do floating panes work (z-order, focus, overlap)?
- How do stacked panes work (tab-like panes within a split)?

### Layout serialization (KDL)
- Zellij uses KDL format for layout definitions
- How are layouts declared, applied, and modified at runtime?
- Can layouts be saved/restored? (relevant to wa-rsaf)

### Pane abstraction
- What is a "pane" in Zellij's type system?
- Plugin panes vs terminal panes vs command panes
- How is pane output captured and streamed?

### Performance under many panes
- How does Zellij handle 50+ panes (relevant to agent swarms)?
- Resource allocation per pane
- Output throttling / backpressure for inactive panes

## FrankenTerm implications
- Can we adopt floating panes for agent tool output?
- Can stacked panes help with agent swarm density?
- Can KDL layouts improve our snapshot/restore?
- Performance lessons for many-pane scenarios?

## Acceptance criteria
- Layout model fully documented (data structures, algorithms)
- Floating and stacked pane mechanics analyzed
- KDL layout format documented
- Many-pane performance characteristics analyzed
- 3+ actionable recommendations for FrankenTerm

## Cross-references
- **wa-2dd4s.2** (floating panes): Zellij's floating pane implementation (z-order management, focus semantics, overlap handling) directly informs FrankenTerm's floating pane design. Document the data structures and algorithms used for floating pane positioning.
- **wa-2dd4s.3** (swap layouts): Zellij's KDL-based layout system and runtime layout modification directly inform FrankenTerm's swap layout feature. Document how Zellij transitions between layout configurations without losing pane state.

## Notes
- Findings from this analysis directly inform the porting of Zellij-style layout features into FrankenTerm. The floating pane and stacked pane patterns are especially relevant for agent swarm UX.
- The many-pane performance analysis is critical: FrankenTerm's agent swarm use case (50+ panes) pushes layout engines harder than typical terminal multiplexer workloads.","notes":"Completed deep code-level layout-engine analysis and delivered evidence dossier at evidence/zellij/layout-engine-analysis.md. Includes topology model, floating/stacked mechanics, KDL parsing/validation, save/restore path, many-pane scaling assessment, and 6 actionable FrankenTerm recommendations with file/line citations.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:09.703614Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:36:16.352191Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:36:16.352127Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1xk9j","depends_on_id":"wa-okyhm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:56.751585Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xk9j","depends_on_id":"wa-22o1q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:02.17403Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-1yk8","title":"Data model: event annotations + triage state","description":"## What
Extend storage schema for event notes, labels, and triage state.

## Why
Without persistence, annotations are lost and triage workflows stay manual.

## How
- Add columns: triage_state, triage_updated_at, triage_updated_by
- Add separate table for labels/notes if needed for many-to-one
- Migrations + indexes for label/state filters

## Risks
- Schema migration must preserve existing events

## Success Criteria
- Storage APIs support add/update/read annotations and labels
- Indexes keep label/state filters fast","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:03:12.890296493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:17:57.865172-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:17:57.865176-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yk8","depends_on_id":"wa-y6g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"wa-1yz79","title":"Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx","description":"# Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx

## Background
Time operations in asupersync go through the Cx capability context. There is no ambient tokio::time::sleep — you must have a Cx.

## Scope
- tokio::time::sleep(dur) → cx.sleep(dur)
- tokio::time::timeout(dur, fut) → asupersync::combinator::timeout(dur, fut) or Budget::deadline
- tokio::time::Instant → asupersync time types

## Asupersync time model
- Uses Budget for deadlines: Budget { deadline, poll_quota, cost_quota, priority }
- Budgets compose via meet operation (tightest constraint wins)
- LabRuntime provides virtual time for deterministic testing

## Affected code patterns
```rust
// Before
tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), operation).await?;

// After
cx.sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
let budget = Budget::deadline(Duration::from_secs(5));
cx.with_budget(budget, operation).await?;
```

## Key locations
- mux_client.rs — connect/read/write timeouts (5s each)
- pool.rs — idle eviction timers, acquire timeouts
- runtime.rs — polling intervals, adaptive sleep
- tailer.rs — capture timeouts
- ipc.rs — request timeouts

## Acceptance criteria
- All tokio::time usage replaced
- Timeouts work correctly under asupersync Budget model
- LabRuntime virtual time works for testing time-dependent code
- No timing regression in connection pool or mux client

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for timer precision**: Add `benches/timer_precision.rs` measuring:
  - cx.sleep() wake-up latency (delta between requested and actual wake time)
  - Budget::deadline accuracy under load (1ms, 10ms, 100ms, 1s deadlines)
  - Timer creation/cancellation throughput (timers created and dropped per second)
  - Comparison with tokio::time::sleep for equivalent operations

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for timer ordering invariants**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary sets of timers with random durations and verify:
  - Timers fire in monotonically non-decreasing order of their deadlines
  - Budget::meet correctly selects the tightest constraint from any pair of budgets
  - Composed budgets never allow execution beyond the earliest deadline
  - No timer starvation: all timers eventually fire if the runtime advances time

## LabRuntime note
- **Deterministic time for testing**: All timer tests should include a LabRuntime variant that uses virtual time. Virtual time tests verify identical ordering semantics without wall-clock jitter. This is critical for CI stability — real-time timer tests are inherently flaky; LabRuntime virtual time tests are deterministic and instant.","notes":"Starting work: remove direct tokio::time usage incrementally.

Phase 1: Replace tokio::time::sleep + tokio::time::timeout callsites with frankenterm_core::runtime_compat::{sleep, timeout} (asupersync-runtime uses asupersync::time under the hood).

Phase 2: Handle remaining tokio::time::interval / Instant / pause sites (either add runtime_compat wrappers or refactor callsites to sleep-loops + LabRuntime virtual time where applicable).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleLantern","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:31.035559Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T21:44:47.382408Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yz79","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:57.700204Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

## Background
Time operations in asupersync go through the Cx capability context. There is no ambient tokio::time::sleep — you must have a Cx.

## Scope
- tokio::time::sleep(dur) → cx.sleep(dur)
- tokio::time::timeout(dur, fut) → asupersync::combinator::timeout(dur, fut) or Budget::deadline
- tokio::time::Instant → asupersync time types

## Asupersync time model
- Uses Budget for deadlines: Budget { deadline, poll_quota, cost_quota, priority }
- Budgets compose via meet operation (tightest constraint wins)
- LabRuntime provides virtual time for deterministic testing

## Affected code patterns
```rust
// Before
tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), operation).await?;

// After
cx.sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
let budget = Budget::deadline(Duration::from_secs(5));
cx.with_budget(budget, operation).await?;
```

## Key locations
- mux_client.rs — connect/read/write timeouts (5s each)
- pool.rs — idle eviction timers, acquire timeouts
- runtime.rs — polling intervals, adaptive sleep
- tailer.rs — capture timeouts
- ipc.rs — request timeouts

## Acceptance criteria
- All tokio::time usage replaced
- Timeouts work correctly under asupersync Budget model
- LabRuntime virtual time works for testing time-dependent code
- No timing regression in connection pool or mux client

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for timer precision**: Add `benches/timer_precision.rs` measuring:
  - cx.sleep() wake-up latency (delta between requested and actual wake time)
  - Budget::deadline accuracy under load (1ms, 10ms, 100ms, 1s deadlines)
  - Timer creation/cancellation throughput (timers created and dropped per second)
  - Comparison with tokio::time::sleep for equivalent operations

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for timer ordering invariants**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary sets of timers with random durations and verify:
  - Timers fire in monotonically non-decreasing order of their deadlines
  - Budget::meet correctly selects the tightest constraint from any pair of budgets
  - Composed budgets never allow execution beyond the earliest deadline
  - No timer starvation: all timers eventually fire if the runtime advances time

## LabRuntime note
- **Deterministic time for testing**: All timer tests should include a LabRuntime variant that uses virtual time. Virtual time tests verify identical ordering semantics without wall-clock jitter. This is critical for CI stability — real-time timer tests are inherently flaky; LabRuntime virtual time tests are deterministic and instant.","notes":"2026-02-12: Phase 1 progress: replaced tokio::time::sleep/timeout with crate::runtime_compat::{sleep, timeout} in pool.rs, tailer.rs, protocol_recovery.rs, workflows.rs; pushed commit b35ec31b.

Next: continue replacing remaining tokio::time callsites across repo; handle interval/Instant/pause in Phase 2.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleLantern","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:31.035559Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T23:16:08.085602Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yz79","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:57.700204Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-20fw","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-3gon (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux
- Rust feature flags: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:14.103113431Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:05.65303Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:05.653011Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-20fw","depends_on_id":"wa-2xe4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-20gu","title":"FTUI-05.2.a Home view parity checklist and intentional delta ledger","description":"## Background
Home view migration needs explicit parity tracking to avoid silent UX regressions during frankentui adoption.

## Deliverables
- widget-by-widget parity checklist (layout, indicators, actions, refresh behavior)
- intentional-delta ledger with justification and user-impact notes
- evidence links to snapshots and test outputs for each checklist item

## Acceptance Criteria
- every Home widget state has either parity confirmation or approved intentional delta
- checklist references deterministic snapshot and unit-test artifacts
- failure notes include reproducible steps and relevant logs for debugging.,","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:16.495001658Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T04:33:12.149218009Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T04:33:12.14908932Z","close_reason":"Wrote evidence/ftui-05.2/matrix.md with 16-row parity matrix: 14 pass, 1 intentional-delta (D4: layout strategy), 0 fail, 0 untested. Covers title, health badge, system status (4 indicators), metrics (pane/event/unhandled/triage counts), loading state, quick help, zero-height safety, minimum-height rendering, and manual refresh lifecycle.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-20gu","depends_on_id":"wa-3pc9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:16.516338961Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-20gu","depends_on_id":"wa-2i6m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:59.74365177Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
